eb4caf121403ca74.summary: When combining solids (cone on cylinder, hemisphere on cylinder), total surface area = sum of visible curved/plane surfaces (excluding internal common surfaces). Only external surfaces count.

chunks.title: Understanding Surface Area of a Combination of Solids
chunks.content:
**Surface Area of a Combination of Solids** is a key concept in Surface Areas and Volumes of CBSE Class X Mathematics.

Many objects are combinations: tent (cone+cylinder), toy (hemisphere+cone), capsule (cylinder+2 hemispheres).

**Key Rule:** Only visible surfaces count. Common faces where solids join are NOT included in TSA.

**Approach:**
1. Identify all component solids
2. List their visible surface areas
3. Sum them (excluding any shared surfaces)

**Key points to remember:**
• Common surfaces excluded from TSA
• Identify each component's contribution
• Sketch and label dimensions
• Use correct formulas for each solid

chunks.title: Properties and Applications of Surface Area of a Combination of Solids
chunks.content:
**1. Properties:**
• Tent = CSA(cone) + CSA(cylinder)
• Toy = CSA(hemisphere) + CSA(cone)
• Capsule = CSA(cylinder) + 2×CSA(hemisphere)
• Any base area included only if visible

**2. Important Formulas:**
TSA = Σ(visible surfaces)
Common surface: excluded once
Cone(CSA) = πrl
Cylinder(CSA) = 2πrh
HS(CSA) = 2πr²

**3. Step-by-Step Approach:**
• Identify the given information
• Apply the appropriate formula
• Solve step by step
• Verify the answer

**4. Example:**
Toy: hemisphere(r=3.5)+cone(r=3.5,h=12.5). l=√(156.25+12.25)=√168.5≈13. TSA=2πr²+πrl=77+143=220 cm².

chunks.title: Worked Example
chunks.content:
**Problem:** A tent: cylinder (r=7m, h=10m) with conical top (r=7m, h=6m). Find canvas required (exclude base).

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** l_cone = √(36+49) = √85 ≈ 9.22 m
**Step 2:** CSA_cyl = 2×22/7×7×10 = 440 m²
**Step 3:** CSA_cone = 22/7×7×9.22 = 202.84 m²
**Step 4:** Total canvas = 440+202.84 = 642.84 m²

Therefore, Canvas ≈ 642.84 m².

chunks.title: Common Mistakes and Tips
chunks.content:
**Common Mistakes:**

❌ **Counting shared surface:** Where solids join, that face is INTERNAL — exclude

**Tips and Tricks:**

✓ **List visible surfaces:** Name each external surface and its formula

chunks.title: Practice Problems
chunks.content:
**Problem 1 (Basic):**
Toy: hemisphere(r=3.5)+cone(r=3.5,h=9.5). Find TSA.

**Answer:** l=√(90.25+12.25)=10.12. TSA=2πr²+πrl=77+111.32=188.32 cm².

---

**Problem 2 (Intermediate):**
Capsule: cylinder(r=2.1,h=7)+2 hemispheres. Find TSA.

**Answer:** TSA=2πrh+4πr²=2×22/7×2.1×7+4×22/7×4.41=92.4+55.44=147.84 cm².

---

**Problem 3 (Advanced):**
Paint tent (cyl r=7, h=8 + cone r=7, h=4.5) at ₹20/m².

**Answer:** l=√(20.25+49)=8.32. CSA=352+183.04=535.04. Cost=₹10700.80.

506049d56b0e5aa4.summary: For combined solids, the total volume is simply the sum of individual volumes: V_total = V₁ + V₂ + V₃ + ... No surfaces are excluded when adding volumes, unlike surface area.

chunks.title: Understanding Volume of a Combination of Solids
chunks.content:
**Volume of a Combination of Solids** is a key concept in Surface Areas and Volumes of CBSE Class X Mathematics.

Unlike surface area, volume calculation for combined solids is straightforward: **add the volumes** of each component.

**Common combinations:**
• Ice-cream cone: V = V_cone + V_hemisphere
• Tent: V = V_cylinder + V_cone
• Rocket: V = V_cone + V_cylinder + V_cone
• Grain silo: V = V_cylinder + V_hemisphere

**Key points to remember:**
• Volume adds directly — no subtraction
• Use correct formula for each solid
• Ensure units are consistent
• 1 m³ = 1000 L for capacity problems

chunks.title: Properties and Applications of Volume of a Combination of Solids
chunks.content:
**1. Properties:**
• Cone V = ⅓πr²h
• Cylinder V = πr²h
• Sphere V = 4/3πr³
• Hemisphere V = ⅔πr³
• Cuboid V = lbh

**2. Important Formulas:**
V_total = V₁ + V₂ + V₃ + ...
All volume formulas as above

**3. Step-by-Step Approach:**
• Identify the given information
• Apply the appropriate formula
• Solve step by step
• Verify the answer

**4. Example:**
Ice-cream cone: cone(r=3,h=12)+hemisphere(r=3). V=⅓π×9×12+⅔π×27=36π+18π=54π≈169.65 cm³.

chunks.title: Worked Example
chunks.content:
**Problem:** A toy: hemisphere(r=3.5) surmounted by cone(r=3.5, h=9.5). Find total volume.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** V_hemi = ⅔π(3.5)³ = ⅔π×42.875 = 28.583π cm³
**Step 2:** V_cone = ⅓π(3.5)²×9.5 = ⅓π×12.25×9.5 = 38.792π cm³
**Step 3:** Total = 67.375π ≈ 211.68 cm³

Therefore, Total V ≈ 211.68 cm³.

chunks.title: Common Mistakes and Tips
chunks.content:
**Common Mistakes:**

❌ **Volume adds — no exclusion:** Unlike TSA, ALL component volumes are added

**Tips and Tricks:**

✓ **Sum the parts:** V_total = V₁+V₂+...+Vₙ

chunks.title: Practice Problems
chunks.content:
**Problem 1 (Basic):**
Ice cream: hemisphere(r=2.1)+cone(r=2.1, h=7). Find V.

**Answer:** V_hemi=⅔π×9.261=6.174π. V_cone=⅓π×4.41×7=10.29π. Total=16.464π≈51.7 cm³.

---

**Problem 2 (Intermediate):**
Tent: cylinder(r=5, h=5)+cone(r=5, h=6). Find V.

**Answer:** V_cyl=125π. V_cone=50π. Total=175π≈549.78 m³.

---

**Problem 3 (Advanced):**
Toy: cylinder(r=3, h=7)+hemisphere(r=3)+cone(r=3, h=5). Find total V.

**Answer:** V_cyl=63π, V_hemi=18π, V_cone=15π. Total=96π≈301.59 cm³.

4cd11f556cb0ca1a.summary: When a solid is melted and recast into another shape, volume remains constant. V_original = V_new. Only volume is conserved — surface area generally changes. Used for sphere→cylinder, cone→sphere, etc.

chunks.title: Understanding Conversion of Solid from One Shape to Another
chunks.content:
**Conversion of Solid from One Shape to Another** is a key concept in Surface Areas and Volumes of CBSE Class X Mathematics.

**Volume Conservation Principle:**
When a solid is melted and recast, the volume remains unchanged.

V_original = V_new

**Common conversions:**
• Sphere → Cylinder
• Cone → Sphere
• Cylinder → Cone(s)
• Multiple smaller spheres → One larger sphere

Surface area CHANGES — only volume is conserved.

**Key points to remember:**
• Volume stays same during conversion
• Surface area generally changes
• Assume no material loss
• Use V equality to find unknown dimensions

chunks.title: Properties and Applications of Conversion of Solid from One Shape to Another
chunks.content:
**1. Properties:**
• Sphere melted → cylinder: 4/3πR³ = πr²h
• Cuboid melted → cube: lbh = a³
• Cone melted → sphere: ⅓πr²h = 4/3πR³
• Number of smaller spheres = (R/r)³

**2. Important Formulas:**
V_original = V_new
V_sphere = 4/3πr³
V_cylinder = πr²h
V_cone = ⅓πr²h
V_cuboid = lbh

**3. Step-by-Step Approach:**
• Identify the given information
• Apply the appropriate formula
• Solve step by step
• Verify the answer

**4. Example:**
Sphere r=3 cm melted to cylinder r=2 cm. Find h.
4/3π×27 = π×4×h → 36 = 4h → h = 9 cm.

chunks.title: Worked Example
chunks.content:
**Problem:** A metallic sphere of radius 4.2 cm is melted and recast into a cylinder radius 6 cm. Find height.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** V_sphere = 4/3π(4.2)³ = 4/3π×74.088 = 98.784π cm³
**Step 2:** V_cyl = π×36×h = 36πh
**Step 3:** 98.784π = 36πh
**Step 4:** h = 98.784/36 = 2.744 cm

Therefore, h ≈ 2.74 cm.

chunks.title: Common Mistakes and Tips
chunks.content:
**Common Mistakes:**

❌ **Conservation = Volume only:** Surface area CHANGES during recasting

**Tips and Tricks:**

✓ **Set volumes equal:** V_original = V_new, solve for unknown

chunks.title: Practice Problems
chunks.content:
**Problem 1 (Basic):**
Cube of edge 7 cm melted to form sphere. Find radius.

**Answer:** 7³=343. 4/3πr³=343 → r=∛(343×3/(4π))≈4.34 cm.

---

**Problem 2 (Intermediate):**
Cone r=6, h=8 melted to sphere. Find sphere radius.

**Answer:** V=⅓π×36×8=96π. 4/3πr³=96π → r³=72 → r≈4.16 cm.

---

**Problem 3 (Advanced):**
Three spheres r=3,4,5 cm melted to one. Find radius.

**Answer:** Total V=4/3π(27+64+125)=4/3π×216. r³=216 → r=6 cm.

cf6c81f20d45f72e.summary: Frustum of a cone: formed by cutting a cone parallel to its base. CSA = π(r₁+r₂)l, TSA = π(r₁+r₂)l+π(r₁²+r₂²), V = ⅓πh(r₁²+r₂²+r₁r₂), where l = √(h²+(r₁-r₂)²).

chunks.title: Understanding Frustum of a Cone
chunks.content:
**Frustum of a Cone** is a key concept in Surface Areas and Volumes of CBSE Class X Mathematics.

A **frustum** is formed when a cone is cut by a plane parallel to its base.

**Parameters:**
h = vertical height
l = √(h²+(r₁-r₂)²)
r₁ = lower base radius
r₂ = upper base radius

**Formulas:**
CSA = π(r₁+r₂)l
TSA = π(r₁+r₂)l+π(r₁²+r₂²)
V = ⅓πh(r₁²+r₂²+r₁r₂)

**Key points to remember:**
• Frustum = cone with tip cut off
• l² = h²+(r₁-r₂)²
• If r₁=r₂, frustum = cylinder
• Volume formula: average of 3 products

chunks.title: Properties and Applications of Frustum of a Cone
chunks.content:
**1. Properties:**
• CSA = π(r₁+r₂)l
• V = ⅓πh(r₁²+r₂²+r₁r₂)
• Original cone height = h×r₁/(r₁-r₂)
• Can also subtract small cone V from large

**2. Important Formulas:**
l = √(h²+(r₁-r₂)²)
CSA = π(r₁+r₂)l
TSA = π(r₁+r₂)l+π(r₁²+r₂²)
V = ⅓πh(r₁²+r₂²+r₁r₂)

**3. Step-by-Step Approach:**
• Identify the given information
• Apply the appropriate formula
• Solve step by step
• Verify the answer

**4. Example:**
Bucket r₁=15, r₂=10, h=12. V=⅓π×12(225+100+150)=4π×475=1900π≈5971.43 cm³.

chunks.title: Worked Example
chunks.content:
**Problem:** A frustum r₁=14 cm, r₂=7 cm, h=9 cm. Find CSA.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** l = √(81+49) = √130 ≈ 11.4 cm
**Step 2:** CSA = π(14+7)×11.4 = π×21×11.4 = 22/7×239.4 = 752.4 cm²

Therefore, CSA ≈ 752.4 cm².

chunks.title: Common Mistakes and Tips
chunks.content:
**Common Mistakes:**

❌ **l not same as h:** Slant height = √(h²+(r₁-r₂)²)

**Tips and Tricks:**

✓ **Check units:** All radii in same units

chunks.title: Practice Problems
chunks.content:
**Problem 1 (Basic):**
Find l of frustum r₁=12, r₂=8, h=3.

**Answer:** l=√(9+16)=5 cm.

---

**Problem 2 (Intermediate):**
Find CSA of frustum r₁=21, r₂=14, h=24.

**Answer:** l=√(576+49)=25. CSA=22/7×35×25=2750 cm².

---

**Problem 3 (Advanced):**
Find V of bucket r₁=20, r₂=15, h=18 (in litres).

**Answer:** V=⅓π×18(400+225+300)=6π×925=5550π≈17442.86 cm³≈17.44 L.
